coredata.dc:title: CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid with good stability in the application of spectral beam splitters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Dmour, Ammar
Xiao, Yang
Tian, Wenshuang
Qin, Guangzhao
Zheng, Xiong
coredata.dc:description: In photovoltaic power systems, nanofluids are promising candidates for spectral beam splitters (SBSs) owing to their excellent spectral absorption regulation characteristics. The drawbacks of high cost and instability restrain the practical application of nanofluid SBSs. Here, we proposed a hybrid nanofluid SBS containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and antimony tin oxide (ATO) nanoparticles, which has been proven to be an excellent SBS from multiple perspectives. Both CQDs and ATO nanoparticles have low cost and super stability, which ensures the long-term practical application of hybrid nanofluid SBSs. Then, the light absorption characteristics of hybrid nanofluids can be effectively regulated because of the complementary optical properties of CQDs and ATO nanoparticles. Furthermore, the PV/T conversion model and indoor test were used to assess the filtering performance of hybrid nanofluid SBSs. The results indicate that CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluids can achieve a high merit function value of 1.815, which is significantly higher than pure CQD nanofluids and outperforms other nanofluid SBSs reported in the literature. This paper may promote the practical utilization of nanofluid SBSs in photovoltaic power systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofluids
Antimony tin oxide
Carbon quantum dots
Spectral beam splitters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrical and thermal efficiencies of nanofluid SBSs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52006059 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scheme and (b) physical picture of the PV/T performance assessment system; (c) Energy spectrum of the solar simulator.

Morphology and stability of hybrid nanofluids. TEM images of CQDs (a) and ATO nanoparticles (b); Diameter distributions of CQDs (c) and ATO nanoparticles(d); Absolute Zeta potentials of CQD and ATO nanofluids before and after heating at 80 °C (e); Transmittance curves of CA-100-10 sample freshly made and put for 3 months (f); Physical pictures of hybrid nanofluids at different mass ratios freshly made (g1) and put for 30 days (g2).
Transmittance curves of CQD nanofluid (CA-100-0) and ATO nanofluid (1000 ppm) (a), hybrid nanofluids with ATO concentrations of 0 ppm (b), 200 ppm (c), 500 ppm (d), and 1000 ppm (e).
The filtering efficiency of CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid SBSs (thickness 10 mm) for PV cell (650–1050 nm).
I–V curves (a) and electrical efficiencies (b) of PV cell with nanofluid SBSs; Temperature rises (c) and thermal efficiencies (d) of nanofluid SBSs.
Total efficiency (a) and MF of PV/T systems at different conditions (b); Comparison between literary results and our work (c).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: c ATO is ATO's concentration in the hybrid nanofluids.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The filtering efficiency of CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid SBSs (thickness 10 mm) for PV cell (650–1050 nm).
MF, electrical efficiency, and thermal efficiency in PV/T systems when w = 3.

head.ce:title: CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid with good stability in the application of spectral beam splitters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan, 410082, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In photovoltaic power systems, nanofluids are promising candidates for spectral beam splitters (SBSs) owing to their excellent spectral absorption regulation characteristics. The drawbacks of high cost and instability restrain the practical application of nanofluid SBSs. Here, we proposed a hybrid nanofluid SBS containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and antimony tin oxide (ATO) nanoparticles, which has been proven to be an excellent SBS from multiple perspectives. Both CQDs and ATO nanoparticles have low cost and super stability, which ensures the long-term practical application of hybrid nanofluid SBSs. Then, the light absorption characteristics of hybrid nanofluids can be effectively regulated because of the complementary optical properties of CQDs and ATO nanoparticles. Furthermore, the PV/T conversion model and indoor test were used to assess the filtering performance of hybrid nanofluid SBSs. The results indicate that CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluids can achieve a high merit function value of 1.815, which is significantly higher than pure CQD nanofluids and outperforms other nanofluid SBSs reported in the literature. This paper may promote the practical utilization of nanofluid SBSs in photovoltaic power systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, fossil fuels occupy the main source of energy for human society, and the combustion of fossil fuels causes serious energy crises and environmental pollution [1–3]. Renewable energy has the advantages of being clean, environmental protection, renewable, etc., and their utilization is promising to mitigate energy and environmental issues caused by fossil fuels [4,5]. In the family of renewable energies, solar energy has been regarded as the direction of future energy development, and many countries focus their eyes on the research and exploration of solar energy [6–11]. As the most successfully commercialized solar energy technology, photovoltaic (PV) technology has developed rapidly in the past few decades, but it also faces some limitations in practice. At present, PV cells can only convert sunlight at a specific wavelength range into electricity and convert sunlight of other wavelengths into heat, so current silicon PV cells have a limited efficiency of 25% [12,13]. Under such conditions, the temperature rise of PV cells under irradiance is inevitable, which is detrimental to the enhancement of the energy utilization efficiency of PV cells. On one hand, the temperature rise of PV cells causes an inhibition effect in energy conversion efficiency. On the other hand, the temperature rise may lead to PV cells damage because of the existence of thermal stress [14,15]. Meanwhile, the heat on PV cells will eventually dissipate into the surrounding environment, which causes serious energy loss.

To improve energy efficiency and avoid thermal stress damage to PV cells, cooling systems are usually brought in PV cells to transfer heat to the desired location, which can effectively suppress the temperature rise of PV cells [16–18]. This strategy is known as traditional photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) technology. Because of the limited temperature rise of PV cells, traditional PV/T systems can only heat the heat exchange fluids (HTFs) to around 315K. This low-grade heat can achieve a low thermal efficiency of about 15% [19], which can only meet the applications of heat pumps, building heating, hot water supply, and other fields [20].

To further raise the energy conversion quality of PV cells, a new type of PV/T system is proposed, which covers the surface of photovoltaic cells with spectral beam splitters (SBSs) to achieve solar spectral utilization. The function of SBS is to allow the sunlight at the wavelengths needed by PV cells to pass through and convert other sunlight into heat. The initial concept of SBSs can be traced back to 1955 when Jackson first proposed this viewpoint [21], and then Moonetal et al. experimented on SBS in 1978 [22]. SBSs can be divided into two types, including fluid-based filters and film based-filters. Typically, fluid-based SBSs are cheaper than film-based ones, so extensive research has been conducted on the former ones in the literature [23]. For fluid-based SBSs, traditional fluids, such as pure oil and water, were used as SBSs in the beginning, while pure fluids exhibit weak absorption in the ultraviolet and infrared regions, making them not good SBSs. In the past few years, nanofluids containing nanoparticles with good optical properties have been regarded as good candidates for SBSs due to the tunable light absorption abilities of nanoparticles.

In literature, some significant research has been performed using various nanofluid SBSs, like metal nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles, polymer-based nanoparticles, core-shell structured nanoparticles, carbon-based nanoparticles, etc. Cui et al. synthesized MgO aqueous nanofluids and achieved a merit function (MF) of 1.55 [24]. Huaxu et al. prepared a glycol nanofluid with the dispersion of ZnO nanoparticles and systematically investigated the influence of glass tube radius on the performance of concentrating PV/T systems, and an MF of 0.867 was obtained in the outdoor experiments [25]. An et al. creatively synthesized a polymer-based nanofluid containing polypyrrole nanoparticles into quartz tube SBSs, which can achieve a high MF of 0.948 in outdoor experiments [26]. Meanwhile, An et al. further improved the performance of SBSs by using Cu9S5/oleylamine nanofluids and the MF value had been increased to 1.415 [27]. Besides, the plasmonic nanoparticles, such as Au [28] and Ag [29] were also applied as SBSs. Even though Au and Ag nanoparticles show good stability in aqueous solutions, the relatively weak absorption of ultraviolet light limits their filtering quality, and the high cost of Au and Ag also limits their large-scale applications. Besides, core-shell structured nanoparticles synthesized through complex processes, like Ag@SiO2, Ag@TiO2 [30–32], can significantly improve the quality of SBSs. The above literature review indicates that some inexpensive and easily obtainable nanofluids typically exhibit relatively ordinary filtering performance, while some nanofluids with special structures of nanoparticles can achieve good performance, but their high cost and complex processes limit their large-scale application. In addition, stability issues have always been one of the important factors hindering the long-term application of nanofluids. Therefore, seeking nanofluids with affordability, good stability, and good filtering ability has always been an important task in the development of nanofluid-related technologies.

Based on this goal, we innovatively proposed a hybrid nanofluid containing antimony tin oxide (ATO) nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Both ATO nanoparticles and CQDs have superiorities in stability, manufacturing process, cost, and optical properties and have been applied in various areas, such as catalysis, energy conversion, medicine, etc. [6,33]. This work shows that ATO nanoparticles and CQDs have complementary optical properties and their hybrid nanofluids can achieve good spectral selective absorption performance desired by SBSs. This work indicates that the CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid can be used as a good SBS, laying the foundation for its practical application in PV/T systems.

Polyethylene glycol with a molecular weight of 200 (PEG) and glycerol (GL) were supported by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. ATO aqueous solution was synthesized by Shanghai Huzheng Nanotechnology Co., Ltd, the average diameter of ATO nanoparticles is about 15 nm. The initial concentration of ATO solution is 30 wt% and is diluted to the target concentrations by deionized water (DW), which was supplied by a Millipore machine directly (Lichen, CS-10B).

PEG-GL-derived CQDs were synthesized by a microwave heating method, which can cause the local heating of dipole materials and result in the carbonization of samples [34]. The synthesis procedures of CQD nanofluids are illustrated in Fig. 1 . In the synthesis of CQD nanofluids, the heating time was set as 20 min, and different PEG:GL mass ratios were adopted. In this procedure, a microwave oven (Xiaomi, MWBLXE1ACM) was used to heat the prepared precursor solution, and the heating power was set to 700 W. After the synthesis of CQD nanofluids, the hybrid nanofluids were produced by a two-step method. First, CQD nanofluid and ATO nanofluid with a certain concentration were weighed by an electronic balance. Next, mix the weighed nanofluids and stir them with a magnetic stirrer for 30 min. Then, the stirred solution underwent ultrasonic vibration for 2 h. At this point, the hybrid nanofluids containing CQDs and ATO nanoparticles were synthesized successfully. Detailed information on prepared nanofluids is shown in Table 1 . When preparing the precursor solutions, the PEG:GL ratio covers all ranges from 0:100 to 100:0 with a step of 10 wt%. For GL-rich solutions, adding a small amount of PEG can cause significant spectral changes, so the step of concentration change is smaller in GL-rich solutions, and the step of concentration change becomes larger in PEG-rich solutions.

Here, the characteristics of nanofluids were systematically analyzed. First, the morphologies of CQDs and ATO nanoparticles were detected by transmission electron microscope with a model of Talos F200S Super-X. Second, the diameter distributions of CQDs and ATO nanoparticles were measured by a DLS particle-size analyzer with a model of Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern. Third, the stability of nanofluids was accessed in three ways, including sedimentation observation, spectrum change analysis (Hitachi, U4100), and Zeta potential analysis (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano ZS).

The transmittance curves of hybrid nanofluid SBSs were detected by UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer from 200 to 2000 nm, and the filtering quality was analyzed systematically based on the transmittance curves. Besides the qualitative analysis of transmittance curves, a PV/T conversion model was also introduced to quantitatively access the filtering performance of hybrid nanofluid SBSs, the calculation formula is as follows [30].
(1a)
η =
∫ 650 1050
E λ
τ λ
d λ
∫ 650 1050
E λ
d λ
−
∫ 200 650
E λ
τ λ
d λ
∫ 200 650
E λ
d λ
−
∫ 1050 2000
E λ
τ λ
d λ
∫ 1050 2000
E λ
d λ

in which η is the filtering efficiency of nanofluids; Eλ is the normal solar irradiance of AM1.5. The solar spectrum between 200 and 2000 nm was taken into consideration in the calculation because the solar irradiance in wavelengths less than 200 nm and larger than 2000 nm is close to zero [35]. τλ represents the transmittance of nanofluids. For an ideal SBS, the transmittance should be 100% in the ideal window for PV cells and 0 at other wavelengths. Therefore, the value of η for a good SBS should be very close to 100%.

Besides the evaluation through the PV/T conversion model, the filtering performance of nanofluid SBSs was also experimentally assessed by a PV/T evaluation system, the scheme and physical picture of this evaluation system are presented in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). In this system, a monocrystalline silicon PV cell, provided by Sunshine Science, was used as the PV cell. The silicon PV cells have a sensitive wavelength region of 650–1050 nm, the experimental determination shows an electrical efficiency of 15.8% for this PV cell. The geometric dimension of the PV cell is 25 × 25 mm2 and the nanofluid coating has a thickness of 10 mm. A xenon lamp (Model: CEL-S500, Aulight Inc.) was used as the supplier of solar radiation, which can supply a uniform intensity light source. The energy spectrum of this xenon lamp is presented in Fig. 2(c), which means that the energy spectrum agrees well with the standard spectrum of AM1.5. An irradiation meter (Model: CEL-FZ-A, Aulight Inc.) was applied to determine the solar simulator's intensity, and the intensity was set as 100 mW/cm2 for all experiments. The I–V characteristics of the cell were analyzed using a solar module analyzer (Model: PROVA-200A, TES Electrical Electronic), and the uncertainties are 0.001 V for voltage and 0.1 mA for current, respectively. During the experiments, an infrared thermal imager (UNI-T, UTi320) was used to measure the temperature of nanofluids and a platinum resistance (Omega, PR-10) was applied to determine the temperature of PV cell, respectively. Both temperature measurement devices have an uncertainty of 0.2 K.

Here, the uncertainties of the PV/T evaluation system were systematically analyzed to confirm the reliability of the experimental results. We have already provided a detailed uncertainty analysis program in our previous paper and briefly described it here [33]. The combined uncertainty is consisting of five parts, namely solar irradiance intensity, I–V characteristics, the area, temperature, and mass of nanofluids. The combined uncertainty of the experimental system can be evaluated as
(1b)
U c
=
∑
i = 1
5
u i 2

in which U c and u i are the combined uncertainty of the whole system and the uncertainty from the subsystems, respectively, which are presented in Table 2 . The relative combined uncertainty of the PV/T evaluation system is 3.60% by calculation. To verify the effectiveness of uncertainty analysis, we conducted repeated experiments with a deviation of less than 3.0%, indicating that our uncertainty analysis is reliable.

The TEM images of CQDs and ATO nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). To have a better observation, CQD nanofluid was diluted 100 times, and ATO nanofluid was diluted to 1 ppm. Then, copper meshes were immersed into the diluted nanofluids and dried for about 30 min at 60 °C. Fig. 3(a) and (b) show that nanoparticles are well dispersed in both nanofluids. In addition, Fig. 3(c) presents the size distribution of CQD and ATO nanofluids, the sizes of ATO nanoparticles and CQDs are uniformly distributed along the average values. The average diameters of CQDs and ATO nanoparticles are 9.5 and 10.9 nm, respectively.

This paper evaluates the stability of hybrid nanofluids using three methods. Fig. 3(e) presents the absolute Zeta potentials of CQD and ATO nanofluids before and after heating at 80 °C. The absolute Zeta potentials of ATO and CQD nanofluids are 41.0 mV and 49.5 mV, respectively. Usually, the nanofluid is regarded as a stable fluid if the Zeta potential is higher than 30 mV [36], which means that both CQD and ATO nanofluids have ultra-high stability. Because CQD nanofluids were synthesized at high temperatures, so the stability remained unchanged after experiencing a high temperature of 80 °C. Meanwhile, the absolute Zeta potential of ATO nanofluid is 40.1 mV after experiencing a high temperature of 80 °C. Even though the value is slightly lower than that before heating, the stability is still good. Fig. 3(f) shows the transmittance change of newly prepared nanofluid and after 3 months of storage, the transmittance increase is within 6.5%. Besides, Fig. 3(g) presents the precipitation of nanofluids, and the results show that almost no precipitation was found after 30 days. After stability analysis, we can conclude that CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluids have ultra-high stability, which is of great importance for their practical applications.
The excellent absorption properties of CQD and ATO nanofluids determine the quality of the filtering performance of hybrid nanofluid SBSs.

Due to ATO being a wide bandgap material with a direct bandgap (Eg = 3.62eV) [37], it has high transmittance in the visible light region. CQDs are composed of dispersed spherical carbon particles with extremely small sizes (below 10 nm). The structure and composition of CQDs determine that they have strong absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region and long tails in the visible light region [38]. It is precisely due to these factors that CQDs and ATO exhibit excellent optical complementary properties, so that their hybrid nanofluid is expected to achieve good filtering performance. The transmittance curves of nanofluids under different conditions were analyzed systematically, the results are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a) compares the transmittance curves of CQD nanofluid (CA-100-0) and ATO nanofluid (c ATO = 1000 ppm). CQD nanofluid shows excellent absorption when the wavelength is less than 650 nm, while the absorption is relatively weak at wavelengths between 1050 and 1400 nm, which causes a significant negative impact on the filtering performance. Coincidentally, even though the ATO nanofluids exhibit poor absorption at wavelengths less than 650 nm, they show extraordinarily good absorption when the wavelength is longer than 1050 nm. Therefore, CQDs and ATO nanoparticles have good complementary light absorption properties. CQD and ATO can regulate the optical properties of nanofluids, and adjusting their components can achieve optimal filtering performance of SBS nanofluids.

Fig. 4(b)–(e) present the transmittance curves of hybrid nanofluids at various ATO concentrations. Fig. 4(b) shows that pure CQD nanofluids can achieve good absorption below 650 nm when adjusting PEG concentration in precursor solutions, while it is evident that the absorption in the wavelengths between 1050 and 1400 nm enhances little with the variation of PEG concentration. Through the analysis of transmittance curves of CQD nanofluids, we can conclude that CQD nanofluids cannot overcome the poor performance in the infrared region by optimizing the compositions of precursor solutions. It is necessary to introduce additional nanoparticles with good absorption of infrared light. Fig. 4(c), (d), and (e) present the transmittance curves of hybrid nanofluids with the addition of different amounts of ATO nanoparticles. Adding ATO nanoparticles into CQD nanofluids can significantly enhance the absorption of nanofluids in the infrared region, while the absorption is slightly weakened simultaneously in the ultraviolet region. Therefore, there exists an ideal concentration that can simultaneously achieve high transmittance in the wavelength region of 650–1050 nm and high absorption in other regions.

To quantitatively evaluate the filtering efficiency of hybrid nanofluid SBSs based on their transmittance data, Eq. (1) is introduced in this work, and the calculated results are presented in Fig. 5 and Table 3 . For pure CQD nanofluids, the PEG concentration can significantly affect the filtering efficiency, and a maximum efficiency of 19.38% occurs at a PEG concentration of 50 wt%. Meanwhile, adding ATO nanoparticles can further enhance the filtering performance of SBSs. Fig. 5 shows that the addition of ATO nanoparticles can significantly enhance the filtering efficiency in the GL-rich CQD nanofluids, while this impact becomes negative in PEG-rich CQD nanofluids. Besides, the concentration of ATO nanoparticles has different effects on different CQD nanofluids, the optimal filtering efficiency of 24.65% appears when the concentration of ATO nanoparticles is 0.05 wt% and PEG concentration is 50 wt%, which is 27% higher than pure CQD nanofluids. Compared with pure CQD nanofluids, ATO nanoparticles overcome the weak absorption characteristics of CQD nanofluids at 1050–1400 nm, so that the optical properties of CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluids match with the spectral of silicon PV cells better.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical and thermal efficiencies of nanofluid SBSs
ce:section.ce:para: After the analysis of the spectral absorption characteristics of nanofluids, their PV/T performance was assessed through the PV/T evaluation system. Fig. 6 (a) illustrates the I–V and power curves of the cell covered with CA-75-5 nanofluid.

According to I–V curves, the formula of PV cell's electrical efficiencies (η pv) is as
(2)
η pv
=
V opt
⋅
I opt
G ⋅ A ⋅ Δ t
where I opt and V opt are the optimal current and voltage of PV cell at maximum power, which can be calculated using I–V curves; G represents the solar simulator's intensity, which is set as 100 mW/cm2; A is the surface area of SBSs, which is measured to be 12.56 cm2. Fig. 6(b) shows the values of η pv at different conditions. For pure CQD nanofluids (c ATO = 0), the increase of PEG concentration weakens the electrical efficiency, and the value decreases from 14.09% to 5.42% as the increase of PEG concentration from 0 to 100 wt%. Besides, the addition of ATO nanoparticles into CQD nanofluids also weakens electrical efficiency, which decreases from 14.09% to 8.00% with the addition of ATO nanoparticles from 0 to 0.10 wt%. The results indicate that the presence of filters always weakens the electrical efficiency of the PV cells, even though the total efficiency may be improved.

In PV/T systems, part of the light required by PV cells is inevitably filtered out by SBS, which can lead to a decrease in the output power of PV cells, while a considerable portion of the sunlight not needed by the PV cell is converted into thermal energy by SBS simultaneously. That is to say, the electrical efficiency decreases with the presence of nanofluid SBS, while the thermal efficiency typically increases. In this way, the total efficiency of the PV/T system considering the electrical efficiency and the thermal efficiency may be enhanced if the spectral absorption of SBS well matches the response spectrum of the PV cell. To conduct a more comprehensive assessment of hybrid nanofluid SBSs, the thermal efficiency of SBSs was analyzed based on the temperature rise data. Fig. 6(c) shows the temperature rise at different irradiating times for a nanofluid SBS. At the initial moment of illumination (first 10 min), temperature rise and irradiating time have a linear relationship. However, the rate of temperature rise gradually decreases due to the significant heat dissipation in the high-temperature zone. Therefore, the temperature rise data were selected within the first 600 s to eliminate the errors caused by heat dissipation. Based on the data on the temperature rise of nanofluid SBSs, thermal efficiency is able to be calculated as [39].
(3)
η th
=
c f
⋅
m f
⋅ Δ T
G ⋅ A ⋅ Δ t
where m f represents nanofluids' mass; c f represents nanofluids' heat capacity, which is regarded to be the same with base fluids approximately because the concentration of nanoparticles is very low [40] and is obtained from literature data [41,42]; ΔT represents the temperature rise of nanofluids; Δt represents the illuminating time. In this work, the coating thickness is small, so the temperature gradient in the vertical direction is negligible and the temperature distribution in the fluids can be considered uniform. Fig. 6(d) presents the thermal efficiency of nanofluid SBSs under different conditions. In pure CQD nanofluids, the thermal efficiency enhances gradually with the addition of PEG. The thermal efficiency increases from 33.85% to 49.78% with PEG concentration varying from 0 to 100 wt%. Moreover, the thermal efficiency is also enhanced when ATO nanoparticles are added into nanofluids, the value of thermal efficiency is improved to 58.94% when 0.10 wt% ATO nanoparticles are added. In our SBS, the temperature rise of nanofluids is almost 15 K, the collected heat can be applied to building heating, heat pumps, hot water supply, etc.

The results in Section 3.3 show that adjusting the nanofluids' composition can effectively affect the electrical and thermal efficiency in PV/T systems. Therefore, optimizing the conditions of nanofluids to achieve optimal PV/T performance is crucial. In this work, we introduced the merit function (MF) to estimate the filtering performance of SBSs further, the formula is as [43].
(4)
MF =
w ⋅
η pv
+
η th
w ⋅
η
pv , unfiltered
where η pv,unfiltered represents the electrical efficiency of our PV cell without any filtration, which is 15.8% based on the experimental measurement; w represents the worth factor considering the prices of the electricity and heat, the value is considered to be 3 here. The efficiencies, as well as the MF values of PV/T systems, are shown in Fig. 7 and Table 4 . For pure CQD nanofluids, MF and PEG concentration show a parabola relationship with a maximum MF of 1.711, occurring at a PEG concentration of 50 wt%, while the maximum total efficiency happens at 75 wt% PEG concentration, the value is 57.0%. Furthermore, the addition of ATO nanoparticles can effectively influence the MF values of hybrid nanofluid SBSs. When PEG concentration in CQD nanofluids is 0, the MF values show a monotonically ascending relationship with ATO concentration. While ATO concentration does not always has a positive effect on MF value with the existence of PEG in CQD nanofluid, and a parabolic relationship is found between ATO concentration and MF value of nanofluid SBSs when PEG is added into CQD nanofluids. The maximum MF value of 1.815 occurs when the PEG concentration in CQD nanofluid is 75 wt% and ATO concentration is 0.05 wt%. While the highest total efficiency occurs at 75 wt% PEG concentration and 0.05 wt% ATO concentration with a value of 67.41%, where the thermal conversion of nanofluid, rather than photovoltaic conversion of PV cells, dominates the entire energy conversion process.

For nanofluid SBSs, some related works have been carried out in the literature. The comparison between our work and literary achievements is illustrated in Fig. 7(c). MgO-water nanofluids were prepared and the filtering performance was checked by Cui et al., which can achieve a high MF of 1.55 [24]. An et al. proposed a special nanofluid consisting of Cu9S5 nanoparticles and oley lamine base fluid, which has a MF value of 1.415, the weak absorption in ultraviolet and infrared regions causes slight energy loss in the filtering process [27]. Huang et al. synthesized a relatively complex nanofluid containing Ag@SiO2, propylene glycol, and CoSO4, the outstanding complementary optical properties of CoSO4 and Ag@SiO2 resulted in an excellent PV/T performance with an MF of 1.69 [30]. While the addition of Ag@SiO2 nanoparticles in carbon nanotubes-water nanofluids can achieve an MF of 1.35, which is due to the poor filtering quality of base-fluid in this system [32]. To overcome the shortages of the proposed nanofluid SBSs, Xiao et al. proposed a carbon quantum dot nanofluid derived from PEG and GL, which shows a high MF of 1.711 and outperforms other nanofluid SBSs reported in the literature [33]. While based on the spectrum analysis of CQD nanofluid we can see that the relatively weak absorption of CQD nanofluid in the wavelengths between 1050 and 1400 nm restrains its PV/T performance. In our work, the prepared CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid can achieve a good PV/T performance with the MF value of 1.815, which indicates that the appropriate addition of ATO nanoparticles further improves the filtering quality of CQD nanofluid. Thus, we can get the conclusion that the hybrid nanofluid in this work shows competitive advantages in the aspects of cost, manufacturing process, stability, and filtering quality.

Meanwhile, we systematically compare the stability of nanofluids in SBSs from literature and our work. The first type of SBS nanofluids is metal oxide nanofluids, like MgO [24] and ZnO [25] nanofluids. While metal oxide nanofluids usually have poor stability, the results show that ZnO nanofluids have serious sedimentation after 4-h stationary [44]. While our CQD-ATO nanofluid has better stability than them. The second type of SBS nanofluids is metal nanofluids, like Au [28] and Ag [29] nanofluids. The literary results indicate that Au and Ag nanofluids show good stability, the transmittance does not change much and no obvious precipitation is found after stored for 10 days [45]. While they show relatively weak absorption for ultra-violet light, which weakens the filtering performance of them. The filtering performance of our CQD-ATO nanofluids is better than Au and Ag nanofluids. The third type is polymer-based nanofluids, like polypyrrole-water nanofluid [26], which show ultra-high stability and there is no significant difference between the absorption of as-prepared nanofluid and that for the nanofluid stored at 4 °C for six months [46]. While similar to Au and Ag nanofluids, the MF value of polypyrrole-water nanofluid is only 0.948, which is not as good as our CQD-ATO nanofluid. Based on the above analysis we can conclude that our CQD-ATO nanofluid is competitive considering both stability and filtering performance.

In this paper, a hybrid nanofluid composed of CQDs and ATO nanoparticles has been proven to be a good SBS. The effects of CQD synthesis composition and ATO concentration on the filtering quality of nanofluid SBSs were systematically analyzed. The regulation of the PEG-GL mass ratio of CQD nanofluids can achieve high transmittance in the ideal window of PV cells and high absorption in the ultraviolet region, while the low absorption between 1050 and 1400 nm cannot be effectively solved. CQDs and ATO nanoparticles present complementary optical properties, so the addition of ATO nanoparticles can enhance the absorption of CQD nanofluids in the wavelengths between 1050 and 1400 nm and can achieve a high filtering efficiency of 24.65% according to PV/T conversion model analysis, which is much higher than CQD nanofluid. The indoor PV/T evaluation experiments indicate that the hybrid nanofluid containing CQD nanoparticles with 50 wt% PEG concentration and 0.05 wt% ATO nanoparticles has the maximum MF of 1.815, This CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid SBS exhibits good competitiveness in terms of cost, preparation procedure, stability, and filtering quality, and this work may promote the utilization of nanofluid SBSs in PV power stations.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Ammar Dmour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yang Xiao: Investigation, Data curation. Wenshuang Tian: Investigation, Data curation. Guangzhao Qin: Writing – review & editing. Xiong Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “CQD-ATO hybrid nanofluid with good stability in the application of spectral beam splitters”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52006059).
